commits.message:
feat: implement managed DB snapshot & restore (Phases 1-3)

Add per-tenant pg_dump-based snapshots with S3 storage, restore from
snapshot, scheduled backups via cron, snapshot-on-delete safety net,
and automatic expiry pruning.

New files:
- Migration 00073: managed_db_snapshots table + tenant backup columns
- Snapshot queries (CRUD), executor (pg_dump/pg_restore streaming),
  storage (S3/MinIO client), Zod schemas, TypeID (dbsnap_ prefix)
- API controller + service for snapshot create/list/show/delete/restore
- CLI: eve db snapshot, snapshots, restore, backup-status
- Orchestrator crons: snapshot scheduler + pruner
- Reconciler: snapshot-on-delete before db destroy

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

feat: complete managed DB snapshot/restore DR plan (Phase 1-4 gaps)

Close remaining gaps from the DR plan across all four phases:

Phase 1: Install postgresql-client-16 in orchestrator Dockerfile
Phase 2: Add download endpoint (presigned S3 URL), getDownloadUrl service
Phase 3: Manifest backup schema (ManagedDbBackupConfigSchema), reconciler
  syncs backup config to tenant rows, snapshot-on-reset in EnvDbService,
  --skip-snapshot flag on destroy/reset, enhanced backup-status endpoint
Phase 4: Emit system.db.snapshot/restore lifecycle events via EventsService

Also adds: updateTenantBackupConfig + syncTenantBackupConfig DB queries,
resolveBackupConfig() with class-based defaults (p1=off, p2+=daily 02:00),
deployer backup config reconciliation on every deploy.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

chore: close snapshot DR tracking bead

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

docs: fix ingest-agentpack repo path and GitHub org in sister repos

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: snapshot service improvements and scheduler hardening

Enhance managed DB snapshot/restore with expanded service logic,
reconciler robustness, scheduler improvements, and schema fixes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: widen credential_secret_ref type to accept null in restore target

The requireTenant method validates non-null at runtime but TypeScript
doesn't narrow the return type. Accept string | null in the parameter.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

docs: unified inference substrate plan — RunPod serverless + Ollama removal

Comprehensive refactoring plan to replace the Ollama-centric inference system
with a provider-agnostic substrate abstraction. RunPod Serverless as first
substrate with true scale-to-zero, auto-wake, and OpenAI-compat API.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

+# Install postgresql-client for managed DB snapshot operations (pg_dump, pg_restore, psql)

+  && apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends postgresql-client-16 \
+  && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
+

@@ -0,0 +1,412 @@
+# Private Model Inference Substrate
+
+> Status: Idea
+> Created: 2026-03-04
+> Related: [pi-mono-integration.md](./pi-mono-integration.md), [ollama-cluster-managed-models.md](./ollama-cluster-managed-models.md)
+
+## The Problem
+
+Eve app creators want to use private models — self-hosted, fine-tuned, or cost-optimized — in two contexts:
+
+1. **Direct inference** from their apps (via `POST /inference/v1/chat/completions`)
+2. **Agent/job execution** via the pi harness (which supports 20+ providers natively)
+
+Today Eve has a solid Ollama-based inference system: scoped targets, managed models, protocol bridges, admission control, wake-on-demand GPU pools, usage tracking. But Ollama is just one inference backend. The world has converged on the **OpenAI-compatible API** as the universal protocol, and there are now dozens of ways to run private models — all speaking the same wire format.
+
+The question isn't *whether* to support them. It's how to do it without building N separate integrations.
+
+---
+
+## The Landscape
+
+Everything converges on one API: `POST /v1/chat/completions`.
+
+### What Exists Today in Eve
+
+| Layer | What | Status |
+|-------|------|--------|
+| **Inference API** | OpenAI-compatible facade at `/inference/v1/chat/completions` | Built |
+| **Target types** | `ollama_pool`, `external_ollama`, `openai_compat` | Built |
+| **Transport profiles** | `ollama_api`, `openai_compat` | Built |
+| **Managed models** | Platform/org/project scoped, merge semantics | Built |
+| **Protocol bridges** | LiteLLM Anthropic→OpenAI (transparent) | Built |
+| **Provider registry** | Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Z.ai, GMICloud, Together, Groq, OpenRouter, Fireworks | Built |
+| **Admission control** | Per-target concurrency, scoped queuing | Built |
+| **Wake-on-demand** | AWS ASG scale-from-zero for GPU nodes (~65s cold start) | Built |
+| **Usage tracking** | Receipts, `llm.call` events, rate cards, budgets | Built |
+| **Pi harness** | 20+ providers, `models.json` config, multi-provider routing | Built (Phase 1) |
+
+### What Exists Outside Eve
+
+| Platform | Model | API | Scale-to-Zero | GPU/hr |
+|----------|-------|-----|---------------|--------|
+| **RunPod Serverless** | Your models on their GPUs | OpenAI-compat | Yes | $0.40-4.50 |
+| **Modal** | Your models on their GPUs | OpenAI-compat | Yes | $0.40-6.25 |
+| **Together AI** | Their catalog | OpenAI-compat | N/A (managed) | Per-token |
+| **Fireworks AI** | Their catalog | OpenAI-compat | N/A (managed) | Per-token |
+| **Vast.ai** | Your models on peer GPUs | OpenAI-compat | Yes | $0.69-2.79 |
+| **Groq/Cerebras** | Fixed catalog, custom silicon | OpenAI-compat | N/A | Per-token |
+| **Pi-pods** | Your models on any SSH GPU | OpenAI-compat | No (persistent) | Provider rate |
+| **Self-hosted vLLM** | Your models on your metal | OpenAI-compat | Manual | Your cost |
+
+**The pattern**: Every serious inference platform speaks OpenAI `/v1/chat/completions`. Eve already has `openai_compat` as a transport profile. The protocol problem is solved.
+
+### What Pi-Pods Brings
+
+Pi-pods (`@mariozechner/pi-pods`) is an SSH-based orchestrator for vLLM on GPU clouds:
+
+- **One command** to go from bare GPU to serving endpoint: `pi pods setup <name> "ssh root@ip"`
+- **Multi-model per pod**: Each model gets its own vLLM process with round-robin GPU assignment
+- **Provider-agnostic**: Works with RunPod, DataCrunch/Verda, Vast.ai, Lambda, AWS EC2, any Ubuntu+NVIDIA+SSH box
+- **Smart defaults**: Auto-selects tool-calling parsers, tensor parallelism, memory allocation
+- **Model caching**: Persistent storage for HuggingFace model weights across restarts
+- **Already in the ecosystem**: Same monorepo as the pi harness Eve already integrates
+
+---
+
+## The Insight
+
+Eve's inference architecture is already 90% ready. The gap isn't protocol support — every target type speaks OpenAI-compat. The gap is **lifecycle management**: how targets get created, how they wake up, how they scale down, and how app creators self-service this without understanding GPU clouds.
+
+Three capabilities are missing:
+
+1. **Provisioning** — Create GPU-backed inference endpoints on demand
+2. **Lifecycle** — Start, stop, health-check, and auto-scale those endpoints
+3. **Self-service** — Let org/project admins manage their own private inference without platform intervention
+
+---
+
+## Options
+
+### Option A: "Bring Your Own Endpoint" (Minimal)
+
+**Philosophy**: Eve doesn't provision infrastructure. Users bring their own OpenAI-compatible endpoints. Eve provides routing, admission, billing, and RBAC.
+
+**What to build**:
+- CLI commands for target registration: `eve inference target add --name my-vllm --base-url https://... --scope org`
+- Model registration: `eve inference model publish --canonical my-model --target my-vllm`
+- Credential scoping: Org/project secrets for target auth (`inference.my-vllm.api_key`)
+- Health probing: Automatic `/health` or `/v1/models` checks on registered targets
+- Documentation: "How to connect RunPod / vLLM / Modal to Eve"
+
+**What users do**:
+1. Provision their vLLM endpoint (manually, via pi-pods, RunPod dashboard, whatever)
+2. Register it with Eve: `eve inference target add ...`
+3. Use it: `managed/my-model` in jobs, apps, or pi harness
+
+**Effort**: ~2 days. Mostly CLI + docs. The `openai_compat` target type already works.
+
+**Pros**:
+- Zero infrastructure risk — Eve doesn't own GPU machines
+- Works with *any* OpenAI-compatible endpoint today
+- Clean separation of concerns
+- Users keep full control of their GPU spend
+
+**Cons**:
+- Users must provision and manage their own GPU endpoints
+- No scale-to-zero without external tooling
+- Fragmented experience — different setup for each GPU provider
+
+---
+
+### Option B: "Pi-Pods as Provisioning Engine"
+
+**Philosophy**: Use pi-pods as the infrastructure layer for GPU provisioning. Eve orchestrates the lifecycle, pi-pods handles the messy GPU/CUDA/vLLM setup.
+
+**What to build**:
+- **Inference provisioner service**: Calls pi-pods CLI to create/destroy GPU endpoints
+- **Provider credentials**: Store GPU provider credentials (RunPod API key, DataCrunch token) as Eve secrets
+- **Lifecycle hooks**: Auto-provision on first model request, auto-destroy on idle timeout
+- **CLI integration**: `eve inference provision --provider runpod --gpu h100 --model deepseek-r1`
+- **Pi harness model routing**: Generate `models.json` dynamically from Eve's managed model catalog
+
+**Architecture**:
+```
+User: eve inference provision --provider runpod --gpu h100 --model qwen3-coder
+  │
+  ▼
+Eve API: Create provisioning job
+  │
+  ▼
+Pi-pods: ssh root@<runpod-ip> → install CUDA → install vLLM → start model
+  │
+  ▼
+Eve API: Register target (openai_compat) → health probe → status: healthy
+  │
+  ▼
+Apps/Jobs: POST /inference/v1/chat/completions { model: "managed/qwen3-coder" }
+```
+
+**Effort**: ~1 week. Pi-pods does the heavy lifting; Eve wraps it with lifecycle management.
+
+**Pros**:
+- One-command GPU provisioning for users
+- Pi-pods is battle-tested across 6+ GPU providers
+- Reuses Eve's existing target/routing/billing infrastructure
+- Same binary the pi harness already ships with
+- NFS model caching across pods (DataCrunch/Verda)
+
+**Cons**:
+- Dependency on pi-pods (MIT, single maintainer — but clean codebase, forkable)
+- SSH-based provisioning adds latency to cold starts
+- No true serverless scale-to-zero (pods stay running)
+- Requires GPU provider accounts and API keys
+- Eve becomes partially responsible for GPU infrastructure
+
+---
+
+### Option C: "Unified Inference Substrate" (Abstract Everything)
+
+**Philosophy**: Ollama, vLLM, RunPod Serverless, and commercial APIs are all just "inference substrates" — interchangeable backends behind a single abstraction. Eve manages the full lifecycle for all of them.
+
+**What to build**:
+
+**1. Substrate abstraction**:
+```
+InferenceSubstrate
+├── OllamaSubstrate (existing: internal pools, external instances, ASG wake)
+├── VllmSubstrate (new: pi-pods managed, self-provisioned, or BYO)
+├── ServerlessSubstrate (new: RunPod Serverless, Modal — API-provisioned)
+└── ApiSubstrate (new: Together, Fireworks, Groq — no provisioning needed)
+```
+
+**2. Unified lifecycle**:
+| Phase | Ollama | vLLM (pi-pods) | Serverless | Commercial API |
+|-------|--------|----------------|------------|----------------|
+| Provision | K8s StatefulSet / EC2 AMI | `pi pods setup` + `pi start` | REST API (create endpoint) | Register base URL |
+| Health | `/api/health` | `/health` or `/v1/models` | Provider API status | `/v1/models` |
+| Wake | AWS ASG SetDesiredCapacity | SSH + `pi start` | Automatic (serverless) | Always on |
+| Scale down | ASG desired=0 | SSH + `pi stop` | Automatic | N/A |
+| Destroy | Terminate instance | `pi pods destroy` | REST API (delete endpoint) | Remove target |
+| Cost model | GPU-hours (owned) | GPU-hours (rented) | Per-second compute | Per-token |
+
+**3. Intelligent routing**:
+```
+Request: { model: "managed/deepseek-r1" }
+  │
+  ├─ Route policy says: prefer org-vllm
+  │   └─ org-vllm healthy? → route there
+  │
+  ├─ Fallback: platform-runpod-serverless
+  │   └─ Scale from zero, ~200ms cold start
+  │
+  └─ Last resort: together-ai
+      └─ Commercial API, per-token billing
+```
+
+**4. Self-service CLI**:
+```bash
+# App creator provisions private inference
+eve inference create \
+  --name team-inference \
+  --substrate vllm \
+  --provider runpod \
+  --gpu h100 \
+  --models "qwen3-coder-32b, deepseek-r1-distill-70b" \
+  --scope project \
+  --idle-timeout 30m
+
+# Check status
+eve inference status team-inference
+# → healthy, 2 models loaded, 45 min idle, $2.49/hr
+
+# Use from app
+curl $EVE_API_URL/inference/v1/chat/completions \
+  -H "Authorization: Bearer $EVE_TOKEN" \
+  -d '{"model": "managed/qwen3-coder-32b", "messages": [...]}'
+
+# Use from pi harness (auto-configured)
+eve job create --harness pi --model managed/qwen3-coder-32b --prompt "..."
+
+# Scale down (or let idle timeout handle it)
+eve inference stop team-inference
+```
+
+**Effort**: ~2-3 weeks for the substrate abstraction + first two substrates (Ollama is already built; add vLLM/serverless). Incremental from there.
+
+**Pros**:
+- Single mental model for all inference — app creators don't think about backends
+- Intelligent fallback chains (private → serverless → commercial)
+- Unified billing: GPU-hours, compute-seconds, and per-token all normalize to cost-per-request
+- Future-proof: new substrates are just adapter implementations
+- Makes Eve a true "inference platform" not just "Ollama wrapper"
+
+**Cons**:
+- Significant engineering investment upfront
+- More surface area to maintain
+- Risk of over-abstraction if substrate differences matter (e.g., vLLM tool-calling quirks)
+- GPU provider API integrations are each their own project
+
+---
+
+### Option D: "Two-Track" (Recommended)
+
+**Philosophy**: Combine Options A and B. Immediate value from BYO endpoints, then progressively add managed provisioning.
+
+**Track 1 — BYO Endpoint (Week 1)**:
+
+Polish the existing `openai_compat` target flow into a first-class self-service experience:
+
+```bash
+# Register any endpoint
+eve inference target add \
+  --name my-vllm \
+  --type openai-compat \
+  --base-url https://vllm.mycompany.com/v1 \
+  --api-key-secret inference.my-vllm.key \
+  --scope org
+
+# Publish models from that target
+eve inference model add \
+  --target my-vllm \
+  --model qwen3-coder-32b \
+  --display-name "Qwen3 Coder (Private)"
+
+# Verify health
+eve inference target health my-vllm
+
+# Use it everywhere
+eve job create --harness pi --model managed/qwen3-coder-32b ...
+```
+
+This works with vLLM, RunPod, Modal, Together AI, Fireworks, Groq — anything OpenAI-compatible. No Eve-side provisioning needed.
+
+**Track 2 — Pi-Pods Managed Provisioning (Week 2-3)**:
+
+Add lifecycle management via pi-pods for users who want Eve to handle the GPU infrastructure:
+
+```bash
+# Provision GPU endpoint (Eve manages via pi-pods)
+eve inference provision \
+  --provider runpod \
+  --gpu h100 \
+  --model qwen3-coder-32b \
+  --scope project \
+  --idle-timeout 30m
+
+# Eve internally:
+# 1. Creates RunPod instance via API
+# 2. Runs pi-pods setup + start via SSH
+# 3. Registers openai_compat target
+# 4. Starts health probing
+# 5. Configures idle timeout for auto-shutdown
+```
+
+**Track 3 — Pi Harness Model Routing (Week 1, parallel)**:
+
+Complete Phase 2 from the pi harness integration plan — dynamically generate `models.json` from Eve's managed model catalog so the pi harness can natively access all managed models:
+
+```bash
+# This already works for jobs:
+eve job create --harness pi --model managed/deepseek-r1 ...
+
+# Behind the scenes, worker generates:
+# ~/.pi/agent/models.json = [
+#   { provider: "eve-managed", model: "deepseek-r1",
+#     baseUrl: "http://eve-api/inference/v1", apiKey: "..." }
+# ]
+```
+
+This means the pi harness — which supports mid-session model switching — can use private models alongside commercial APIs in the same conversation.
+
+---
+
+## How Private Models Flow Through Eve
+
+### For Apps (Direct Inference)
+
+```
+App Code:
+  fetch(`${EVE_API_URL}/inference/v1/chat/completions`, {
+    headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${EVE_TOKEN}` },
+    body: JSON.stringify({
+      model: 'managed/qwen3-coder-32b',
+      messages: [{ role: 'user', content: '...' }],
+      stream: true
+    })
+  })
+
+Eve resolves:
+  managed/qwen3-coder-32b
+    → managed model catalog (project scope)
+    → target: itgt_orgvllm (type: openai_compat)
+    → base_url: https://vllm.org.runpod.io/v1
+    → admission: check concurrency, queue if needed
+    → forward request → stream response
+    → record usage (tokens, cost, org/project attribution)
+```
+
+### For Jobs/Agents (Pi Harness)
+
+```
+eve job create --harness pi --model managed/qwen3-coder-32b --prompt "..."
+
+Worker:
+  1. Resolve managed/qwen3-coder-32b → target + credentials
+  2. Generate PI_MODELS_JSON_B64 with Eve inference endpoint
+  3. Spawn: pi --model managed/qwen3-coder-32b --mode json ...
+  4. Pi talks to Eve inference API → routes to private vLLM
+  5. Stream JSONL results → execution log
+  6. Extract usage → llm.call events → cost tracking
+```
+
+### For Agent Runtime (Chat)
+
+```
+Slack message → Gateway → Agent Runtime
+  Agent config: { harness: 'pi', model: 'managed/qwen3-coder-32b' }
+
+Agent Runtime:
+  1. Resolve model → Eve inference endpoint
+  2. Pi agent uses managed model for responses
+  3. Mid-conversation: can switch to commercial API if needed
+  4. All usage tracked through Eve's billing system
+```
+
+---
+
+## Relationship to Managed Ollama
+
+Private model infrastructure **complements** Ollama, it doesn't replace it.
+
+| Dimension | Ollama (Current) | Private vLLM/Serverless (Proposed) |
+|-----------|------------------|------------------------------------|
+| **Best for** | Quick experiments, small models, team-shared inference | Production workloads, large models, fine-tuned models |
+| **Model size** | Up to ~70B (single GPU) | Up to ~400B+ (tensor parallelism) |
+| **Provisioning** | Platform-managed (K8s + AMI) | Self-service (BYO or Eve-managed) |
+| **Scaling** | ASG scale-to-zero (65s cold start) | Provider-dependent (200ms - 3min) |
+| **Cost model** | GPU-hours (owned infrastructure) | GPU-hours (rented) or per-token (commercial) |
+| **Control** | Platform admin only | Org/project self-service |
+| **Use case** | Platform default, development, cost-optimized | Org-specific models, data sovereignty, performance |
+
+The unified inference API (`/inference/v1/chat/completions`) routes transparently across both. Users don't need to know which backend serves their request.
+
+---
+
+## Open Questions
+
+1. **Credential scoping**: How do GPU provider API keys (RunPod, DataCrunch) flow through Eve's secrets system? Per-org? Per-project? Platform-managed?
+
+2. **Cost attribution**: GPU-hour costs don't map cleanly to per-token billing. Should Eve normalize everything to per-request cost, or expose the underlying billing model?
+
+3. **Multi-model targets**: Pi-pods supports multiple models per GPU pod. Should Eve's target model be 1:1 (target:model) or 1:N?
+
+4. **Idle timeout ownership**: Who decides when a GPU pod shuts down — the platform (cost control) or the org (availability)?
+
+5. **Fine-tuned model deployment**: Users with fine-tuned HuggingFace models need to specify model IDs, quantization, and context window. How much of this does Eve abstract vs. expose?
+
+6. **Pi-pods dependency**: Is it acceptable to depend on pi-pods for provisioning, or should Eve build its own vLLM orchestration? (Pi-pods is MIT, clean, and forkable — but single maintainer.)
+
+---
+
+## Recommendation
+
+**Start with Option D (Two-Track):**
+
+- **Week 1**: Ship Track 1 (BYO Endpoint) + Track 3 (Pi model routing). This gives app creators immediate access to private models with zero Eve-side infrastructure changes. The `openai_compat` target type already works — just needs CLI polish and docs.
+
+- **Week 2-3**: Ship Track 2 (Pi-Pods provisioning) for users who want Eve-managed GPU inference. This is the "magic" experience — one command to go from nothing to a private model endpoint.
+
+- **Later**: Evaluate whether the substrate abstraction (Option C) is worth the investment. It becomes compelling when Eve has 3+ provisioning backends and users are managing complex fallback chains.
+
+The key architectural principle: **every private model endpoint is just an `openai_compat` target**. Whether it's provisioned by pi-pods, RunPod's API, or manually — Eve sees the same thing. This means Track 1 is not throwaway work; it's the foundation that Track 2 and Option C build on.

+# Managed DB Snapshot & Restore — Disaster Recovery Plan
+
+> Status: Draft
+> Last Updated: 2026-03-05
+> Purpose: Close the DR gap for Eve-managed databases with per-tenant snapshot/restore, scheduled backups via manifest cron, and S3-based persistence.
+
+## Why This Plan
+
+Eve-managed databases (`x-eve.role: managed_db`) currently have no per-tenant backup or restore capability. The system-level RDS instance has 7-day automated backups, but restoring a single tenant DB requires restoring the entire instance — a blunt instrument that disrupts all tenants.
+
+The existing DBaaS plan (Phase 5) calls for "snapshot-before-destroy" but defers the implementation. This plan closes the full DR gap:
+
+1. **On-demand snapshots** — `eve db snapshot` creates a point-in-time `pg_dump` stored in S3.
+2. **Scheduled snapshots** — manifest-level cron triggers periodic backups without app code.
+3. **Restore** — `eve db restore` recovers a tenant DB from any stored snapshot.
+4. **Snapshot-on-delete** — automatic safety net before `eve db destroy` on production-class DBs.
+5. **Lifecycle management** — retention policies, listing, pruning.
+
+---
+
+## 1) Current State
+
+### What Exists
+
+| Layer | Backup Coverage | Granularity |
+|-------|----------------|-------------|
+| RDS automated backups | 7-day retention, 03:00-04:00 UTC window | Entire RDS instance |
+| `eve db reset --force` | Drops and recreates schema | Destructive, no snapshot |
+| `eve db destroy --force` | DROP DATABASE + DROP ROLE | No snapshot before drop |
+| App object store (`x-eve.object_store`) | S3-compatible bucket provisioning | Per-service buckets |
+
+### What's Missing
+
+- No `pg_dump`-based per-tenant snapshots
+- No `eve db snapshot` or `eve db restore` CLI commands
+- No scheduled/cron backup support
+- No snapshot-on-delete in the reconciler
+- No snapshot storage, listing, or retention management
+- No documented RTO/RPO targets
+
+---
+
+## 2) Design Principles
+
+1. **pg_dump over RDS snapshots** — Tenant DBs are logical databases on a shared instance. `pg_dump` gives per-tenant granularity without touching other tenants. Fast, cheap, portable.
+2. **S3 as the snapshot store** — Reuse the existing app object store infrastructure. Platform provisions a system-level snapshot bucket per deployment.
+3. **Manifest-driven scheduling** — App teams declare backup frequency in their manifest. The platform handles execution. No app code required.
+4. **CLI-first** — Every operation is available via `eve db snapshot|restore|snapshots`. Agents and humans use the same interface.
+5. **Production-first defaults** — `db.p2+` classes get daily snapshots and snapshot-on-delete by default. `db.p1` is opt-in only.
+
+---
+
+## 3) Snapshot Storage Model
+
+### 3.1 Storage Location
+
+Snapshots are stored in an Eve-managed S3 bucket, one per deployment:
+
+```
+s3://${deployment_id}-db-snapshots/
+  └── {org_slug}/
+      └── {project_slug}/
+          └── {env_name}/
+              └── {timestamp}_{snapshot_id}.dump
+```
+
+The bucket is provisioned by Terraform in the infra repo (same pattern as the registry bucket `eh1-registry-*`). Eve services access it via IRSA or static credentials depending on the deployment model.
+
+**Local development (k3d)**: Use MinIO (same as app object store). The `BucketProvisioner` in `apps/worker/src/deployer/bucket-provisioner.ts` already supports both `s3` and `minio` backends via `EVE_STORAGE_BACKEND`. The snapshot service should reuse these env vars rather than introducing a parallel configuration path.
+
+### 3.2 Snapshot Metadata
+
+Each snapshot gets a metadata record in the `managed_db_snapshots` table:
+
+```sql
+CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS managed_db_snapshots (
+  id            TEXT PRIMARY KEY,         -- dbsnap_xxx (TypeID, generated in app code via typeid('dbsnap'))
+  tenant_id     TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES managed_db_tenants(id),
+  org_id        TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES orgs(id),
+  project_id    TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES projects(id),
+  env_id        TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES environments(id),
+  instance_id   TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES managed_db_instances(id),
+  created_by    TEXT,                     -- user_id or 'system' for scheduled/pre_delete
+
+  -- Snapshot details
+  trigger       TEXT NOT NULL,            -- 'manual' | 'scheduled' | 'pre_delete' | 'pre_reset'
+  status        TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'in_progress',  -- 'in_progress' | 'completed' | 'failed'
+  s3_bucket     TEXT,                     -- Bucket name (enables multi-deployment portability)
+  s3_key        TEXT,                     -- Full S3 object key
+  size_bytes    BIGINT,                   -- Compressed dump size
+  db_size_bytes BIGINT,                   -- Logical DB size at snapshot time
+  pg_version    TEXT,                     -- Postgres version at snapshot time (for restore compat)
+  error_message TEXT,
+
+  -- Lifecycle
+  retention     TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '30d',  -- Retention duration
+  expires_at    TIMESTAMPTZ,
+  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
+  completed_at  TIMESTAMPTZ,
+
+  CONSTRAINT valid_trigger CHECK (trigger IN ('manual', 'scheduled', 'pre_delete', 'pre_reset')),
+  CONSTRAINT valid_status CHECK (status IN ('in_progress', 'completed', 'failed'))
+);
+
+CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_snapshots_tenant ON managed_db_snapshots(tenant_id);
+CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_snapshots_expires ON managed_db_snapshots(expires_at) WHERE status = 'completed';
+CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_snapshots_tenant_created ON managed_db_snapshots(tenant_id, created_at DESC);
+CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_snapshots_org ON managed_db_snapshots(org_id);
+CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_snapshots_status ON managed_db_snapshots(status) WHERE status = 'in_progress';
+```
+
+> **Implementation note**: The codebase generates TypeIDs in application code via `typeid('dbsnap')` (see `packages/shared/src/ids.ts`), not via SQL `DEFAULT` expressions. Add a `generateManagedDbSnapshotId()` export alongside the existing `generateManagedDbInstanceId()` and `generateManagedDbTenantId()` functions.
+
+### 3.3 Retention Defaults
+
+| DB Class | Default Retention | Snapshot-on-Delete | Default Schedule |
+|----------|------------------|--------------------|------------------|
+| `db.p1`  | 7 days           | Off                | Off (opt-in)     |
+| `db.p2`  | 30 days          | On                 | Daily 02:00 UTC  |
+| `db.p3`  | 90 days          | On                 | Daily 02:00 UTC  |
+
+For tighter `db.p3` recovery targets, set an explicit `backup.schedule` at manifest level.
+
+Override retention per-snapshot via `--retention 90d` on manual snapshots.
+

+
+## 4) Manifest Integration — Scheduled Snapshots
+
+### 4.1 Manifest Schema Extension
+
+Add a `backup` block to the managed DB service config:
+
+```yaml
+services:
+  db:
+    x-eve:
+      role: managed_db
+      managed:
+        class: db.p2
+        engine: postgres
+        engine_version: "16"
+        backup:
+          schedule: "0 2 * * *"       # Standard cron expression (daily at 02:00 UTC)
+          retention: 30d              # How long to keep snapshots
+          snapshot_on_delete: true    # Snapshot before eve db destroy
+          snapshot_on_reset: true     # Snapshot before eve db reset
+```
+
+### 4.2 Defaults by Class
+
+When `backup` is omitted, the platform applies class-based defaults:
+
+- **`db.p1`**: No scheduled backup, no snapshot-on-delete (dev tier).
+- **`db.p2`+**: `schedule: "0 2 * * *"`, `retention: 30d`, `snapshot_on_delete: true`, `snapshot_on_reset: true`.
+
+To explicitly disable on a production class:
+
+```yaml
+backup:
+  schedule: false
+  snapshot_on_delete: false
+```
+
+### 4.3 Per-Environment Override
+
+```yaml
+environments:
+  production:
+    overrides:
+      services:
+        db:
+          x-eve:
+            managed:
+              backup:
+                schedule: "0 */6 * * *"   # Every 6 hours for production
+                retention: 90d
+```
+
+### 4.4 How Scheduling Works
+
+The orchestrator's cron subsystem evaluates managed DB backup schedules:
+
+1. On each cron tick, the orchestrator queries all `managed_db_tenants` with `status = 'ready'` and an active backup schedule.
+2. For each tenant whose schedule is due (based on `last_snapshot_at` and UTC), it creates a snapshot job (internal, not user-visible as a pipeline run).
+3. The snapshot job runs `pg_dump` against the tenant DB, streams the output to S3 via `@aws-sdk/lib-storage` (multipart upload), and updates the snapshot record.
+4. On completion, the cron records the last snapshot time on the tenant record to avoid duplicate runs.
+
+This reuses the same cron infrastructure that drives the usage sweeper, suspension controller, and fx updater.
+
+**Implementation note**: The `managed_db_tenants` table does not currently have `last_snapshot_at` or `backup_schedule` columns. The migration must add:
+
+```sql
+ALTER TABLE managed_db_tenants
+  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS backup_schedule TEXT,          -- cron expression, NULL = no schedule
+  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS backup_retention TEXT,         -- e.g. '30d', NULL = class default
+  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS snapshot_on_delete BOOLEAN,    -- NULL = class default
+  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS snapshot_on_reset BOOLEAN,     -- NULL = class default
+  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS last_snapshot_at TIMESTAMPTZ;  -- set by scheduler after successful snapshot
+```
+
+These columns are populated by the deployment reconciler when it reads the manifest's `backup` block. The scheduler cron queries them directly — no manifest parsing at cron time.
+

+## 5) Snapshot Execution
+
+### 5.1 pg_dump Strategy
+
+```
+PGPASSWORD=<admin_pass> pg_dump --format=custom --compress=6 --no-owner --no-acl \
+  --dbname=<tenant_db> --host=<instance_host> --port=<port> --username=<admin_user>
+```
+
+- **Format**: `custom` (`-Fc`) — supports parallel restore, selective table restore, and is smaller than plain SQL.
+- **Compression**: `--compress=6` in pg_dump custom format for a balanced speed/size ratio.
+- **No owner/ACL**: Tenant DB users are platform-managed; ownership is re-applied on restore.
+- **Streaming upload**: Pipe `pg_dump` stdout directly to S3 multipart upload via `@aws-sdk/lib-storage` `Upload` class. No local temp file needed.
+- **Auth**: Pass credentials via `PGPASSWORD` env var (not `--password`). The admin credentials are derived from the orchestrator's `DATABASE_URL` — same approach used by `connectToInstance()` in the reconciler.
+
+**Node.js implementation**: Use `child_process.spawn('pg_dump', [...args])` and pipe `child.stdout` as a `Readable` stream into the S3 `Upload`. Handle `child.stderr` for error capture and `child.on('exit', code)` for failure detection.
+
+### 5.2 Snapshot Job Flow
+
+```
+1. Create snapshot record (status: in_progress)
+2. Query tenant DB size (`pg_database_size`) and approximate row count (`pg_stat_user_tables`).
+3. Start pg_dump, stream to S3 via multipart upload
+4. On success: update snapshot record (status: completed, size_bytes, completed_at)
+5. On failure: update snapshot record (status: failed, error_message)
+6. Emit event: system.db.snapshot.completed or system.db.snapshot.failed
+```
+
+### 5.3 Concurrency Guards
+
+- Only one snapshot per tenant at a time (checked via `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` on queue rows and `status = 'in_progress'` query guard).
+- Snapshot does not acquire locks beyond `pg_dump`'s default `ACCESS SHARE` — safe to run on live databases.
+- Timeout: 30 minutes per snapshot (covers up to ~50GB compressed). Configurable per class.
+
+---
+
+## 6) Restore Execution
+
+### 6.1 Restore Strategy
+
+```
+pg_restore --format=custom --no-owner --no-acl --clean --if-exists \
+  --dbname=<tenant_db> --host=<instance_host> --port=<port> --username=<admin_user>
+```
+
+- **`--clean --if-exists`**: Drop existing objects before restoring. Safe for full-DB restore.
+- **No owner/ACL**: Re-apply tenant role ownership after restore.
+- **Stream from S3**: Download snapshot directly into `pg_restore` stdin.
+
+### 6.2 Restore Flow
+
+```
+1. Validate snapshot exists and status = 'completed'
+2. Create pre-restore safety snapshot (trigger: 'pre_reset', unless --skip-safety-snapshot)
+3. Terminate active connections to tenant DB
+4. Run pg_restore from S3 snapshot
+5. Re-apply tenant role ownership (GRANT ALL ON ALL TABLES/SEQUENCES/FUNCTIONS)
+6. Verify restore (table count + basic connectivity check)
+7. Emit event: system.db.restore.completed or system.db.restore.failed
+```
+
+### 6.3 Cross-Environment Restore
+
+Restoring a snapshot to a different environment is supported:
+
+```bash
+eve db restore --env staging --snapshot dbsnap_xxx --source-env production [--source-project <id>]
+```
+
+This enables:
+- Cloning production data to staging for debugging
+- Disaster recovery to a different environment
+- Data seeding for new environments
+
+Credentials are remapped to the target tenant's role automatically.
+
+### 6.4 Portable Export & Import
+
+Snapshots are standard `pg_dump` custom-format files. The CLI provides explicit export/import commands for moving data outside the Eve ecosystem:
+
+#### Download (Eve → local file)
+
+```bash
+# Download a snapshot as a portable pg_dump file
+eve db snapshot download <snapshot_id> --output ./myapp-production-20260305.dump
+```
+
+The downloaded file works with any standard `pg_restore`:
+
+```bash
+# Restore to any Postgres instance — no Eve required
+pg_restore --clean --if-exists --no-owner --no-acl \
+  -d postgres://user:pass@external-host:5432/mydb \
+  ./myapp-production-20260305.dump
+```
+
+#### Export (Eve → external Postgres, direct)
+
+```bash
+# Stream a snapshot directly into an external database
+eve db export --snapshot <snapshot_id> --url postgres://user:pass@host:5432/target_db
+  [--clean]                                   # Drop existing objects first
+  [--force]
+```
+
+This pipes S3 → `pg_restore` → external DB in one step. No local file needed.
+
+#### Import (external dump → Eve managed DB)
+
+```bash
+# Import a pg_dump file into an Eve-managed DB
+eve db import --env <name> --file ./external-backup.dump
+  [--project <id>]
+  [--skip-safety-snapshot]                    # Skip pre-import backup
+  [--clean]                                   # Drop existing objects first
+  [--force]
+
+# Import directly from an external Postgres database
+eve db import --env <name> --source-url postgres://user:pass@host:5432/source_db
+  [--project <id>]
+  [--skip-safety-snapshot]
+  [--force]
+```
+
+The `--source-url` variant runs `pg_dump` against the external database and streams directly into the Eve-managed tenant DB. This is the primary path for migrating onto Eve from an existing Postgres deployment.
+
+#### Cross-Instance Migration (Eve → Eve)
+
+For migrating between Eve instances (e.g., staging cluster → production cluster):
+
+```bash
+# On source instance:
+eve db snapshot --env production
+eve db snapshot download dbsnap_xxx --output ./migration.dump
+
+# On target instance:
+eve db import --env production --file ./migration.dump --force
+```
+
+Or, if both instances are network-reachable:
+
+```bash
+# Direct pipe: source Eve snapshot → target Eve managed DB
+eve db export --snapshot dbsnap_xxx --url postgres://user:pass@target-host:5432/target_db
+```
+
+The download/import path is preferred for cross-instance migration since it doesn't require network connectivity between clusters and creates an auditable artifact.
+

+## 8) API Endpoints
+
+These endpoints live in the existing `ManagedDbController` (`apps/api/src/environments/managed-db.controller.ts`), extending the current `/projects/:id/envs/:name/db/managed` route tree.
+
+```
+# Snapshots (permission: envdb:read / envdb:write)
+POST   /projects/:id/envs/:name/db/snapshots              # Create snapshot (envdb:write)
+GET    /projects/:id/envs/:name/db/snapshots               # List snapshots (envdb:read)
+GET    /projects/:id/envs/:name/db/snapshots/:snapshotId   # Show snapshot (envdb:read)
+DELETE /projects/:id/envs/:name/db/snapshots/:snapshotId   # Delete snapshot (envdb:write)
+
+# Restore (permission: envdb:write)
+POST   /projects/:id/envs/:name/db/restore
+       Body: { snapshot_id, source_env?, source_project?, skip_safety_snapshot? }
+
+# Backup status (permission: envdb:read)
+GET    /projects/:id/envs/:name/db/backup-status
+
+# Export / Import (permission: envdb:write)
+GET    /projects/:id/envs/:name/db/snapshots/:snapshotId/download  # Returns signed S3 URL
+POST   /projects/:id/envs/:name/db/export
+       Body: { snapshot_id, target_url, clean? }
+POST   /projects/:id/envs/:name/db/import
+       Body: { source_url?, clean?, skip_safety_snapshot? }
+       (or multipart file upload)
+```
+
+**Permission model**: Reuses the existing `envdb:read` and `envdb:write` permissions from `ManagedDbController`. Snapshot creation, deletion, restore, export, and import require `envdb:write`. Listing and viewing require `envdb:read`.

+## 9) Infrastructure Requirements
+
+### 9.1 S3 Bucket (Terraform — Infra Repo)
+
+Add a `db-snapshots` module to the infra repo:
+
+```hcl
+module "db_snapshots" {
+  source      = "./modules/s3-bucket"
+  bucket_name = "${var.deployment_id}-db-snapshots"
+
+  lifecycle_rules = [
+    {
+      id      = "expire-old-snapshots"
+      prefix  = ""
+      enabled = true
+      expiration_days = 90  # Hard ceiling — app-level retention is typically shorter
+    },
+    {
+      id      = "abort-incomplete-multipart"
+      prefix  = ""
+      enabled = true
+      abort_incomplete_multipart_upload_days = 1  # Clean up failed streaming uploads
+    }
+  ]
+
+  encryption = {
+    sse_algorithm = "aws:kms"
+    kms_key_id    = var.kms_key_id
+  }
+
+  # IRSA access for Eve services
+  iam_role_arn = module.eve_irsa.role_arn
+}
+```
+
+### 9.2 IRSA Policy
+
+Add S3 permissions for the snapshot bucket to the Eve service IRSA role:
+
+```json
+{
+  "Effect": "Allow",
+  "Action": [
+    "s3:PutObject",
+    "s3:GetObject",
+    "s3:DeleteObject",
+    "s3:ListBucket",
+    "s3:AbortMultipartUpload",
+    "s3:ListMultipartUploadParts",
+    "s3:ListBucketMultipartUploads"
+  ],
+  "Resource": [
+    "arn:aws:s3:::${deployment_id}-db-snapshots",
+    "arn:aws:s3:::${deployment_id}-db-snapshots/*"
+  ]
+}
+```
+
+### 9.3 Worker / Orchestrator Images
+
+The worker and orchestrator images need `pg_dump` and `pg_restore` binaries. **These are NOT currently installed** — the worker Dockerfile (`apps/worker/Dockerfile`) uses `node:22-slim` and installs git, kubectl, BuildKit, etc. but not `postgresql-client`.
+
+**Required change** — add to both the worker and orchestrator Dockerfiles:
+
+```dockerfile
+RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends postgresql-client && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
+```
+
+The `postgresql-client` package on Debian Bookworm (node:22-slim base) provides pg_dump/pg_restore for Postgres 15. For Postgres 16 tenant DBs, install from the PGDG repo instead:
+
+```dockerfile
+RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends gnupg lsb-release \
+  && echo "deb http://apt.postgresql.org/pub/repos/apt $(lsb_release -cs)-pgdg main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pgdg.list \
+  && curl -fsSL https://www.postgresql.org/media/keys/ACCC4CF8.asc | gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/pgdg.gpg \
+  && apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends postgresql-client-16 \
+  && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
+```
+
+**Where the snapshot runs**: Since scheduled snapshots and snapshot-on-delete run in the orchestrator process (cron services), the orchestrator image is the primary target. The worker needs the binaries only if snapshots are triggered as worker jobs. For Phase 1, installing in the orchestrator is sufficient.
+

+## 10) Reconciler Changes
+
+### 10.1 Snapshot-on-Delete
+
+In `ManagedDbReconcilerService.handleDeleting()` (`apps/orchestrator/src/cron/managed-db-reconciler.service.ts`):
+
+The current flow calls `deleteLocalDb()` which does: terminate connections → DROP DATABASE → DROP ROLE, then `markTenantDeleted()`.
+
+```
+Before (current):
+  1. deleteLocalDb(instance, tenant):
+     a. Terminate connections
+     b. DROP DATABASE IF EXISTS
+     c. DROP ROLE IF EXISTS
+  2. markTenantDeleted(id)
+
+After:
+  1. Check if snapshot_on_delete is enabled (tenant.snapshot_on_delete or class default)
+  2. If enabled AND tenant.credential_secret_ref exists (DB was actually provisioned):
+     a. Create snapshot (trigger: 'pre_delete'), wait for completion (timeout: 5 min)
+     b. If snapshot fails: log warning, proceed with delete (don't block teardown)
+  3. deleteLocalDb(instance, tenant) — unchanged
+  4. markTenantDeleted(id) — unchanged
+```
+
+**Note**: The reconciler uses CAS-based operation locks (`acquireOperationLock`/`transitionStatus`). The snapshot step runs within the existing lock — no new lock acquisition needed.
+
+### 10.2 Scheduled Snapshot Cron
+
+New cron job in the orchestrator (`ManagedDbSnapshotScheduler`), registered in `CronModule` alongside the existing 6 cron services:
+
+```
+Enable: EVE_MANAGED_DB_SNAPSHOT_SCHEDULER_ENABLED=true
+Cron:   */60 * * * * *  (every minute)
+
+Every tick:
+  1. Guard: if already running, skip (same pattern as ManagedDbReconcilerService)
+  2. Query managed_db_tenants WHERE status = 'ready'
+       AND backup_schedule IS NOT NULL AND deleted_at IS NULL
+  3. For each tenant: evaluate cron expression against last_snapshot_at
+     - Use `cron-parser` (already in deps via the CronSchedulerService)
+  4. If due AND no in_progress snapshot exists for this tenant:
+     - Create snapshot (trigger: 'scheduled')
+  5. Respect max concurrent snapshots per instance (default: 2)
+     - Query COUNT(*) from managed_db_snapshots WHERE instance_id = ? AND status = 'in_progress'
+```
+
+**Pattern reference**: Follow the exact NestJS lifecycle pattern from `ManagedDbSweeperService` — `OnModuleInit`/`OnModuleDestroy`, env-gated enable, `CronJob` from `cron` package, `this.running` overlap guard.
+
+### 10.3 Snapshot Expiry Pruner
+
+New cron job (`ManagedDbSnapshotPruner`):
+
+```
+Enable: EVE_MANAGED_DB_SNAPSHOT_PRUNER_ENABLED=true
+Cron:   0 */1 * * *  (every hour, on the hour)
+
+Every tick:
+  1. Query managed_db_snapshots WHERE expires_at < NOW() AND status = 'completed'
+     LIMIT 100  (batch to avoid long-running transactions)
+  2. For each: delete S3 object (ignore 404 — idempotent)
+  3. Delete snapshot records
+  4. Also clean up failed/stale snapshots: status = 'in_progress' AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '2 hours'
+     (mark as 'failed' with error_message 'timed out')
+  5. Log pruned count per category (expired, stale)
+```
+
+**S3 lifecycle belt-and-suspenders**: The S3 bucket lifecycle rule (90-day hard ceiling) acts as a safety net if the pruner fails. The pruner handles app-level retention (7d/30d/90d per class), while S3 lifecycle handles the absolute maximum.
+

+
+| Event | When | Payload |
+|-------|------|---------|
+| `system.db.snapshot.started` | Snapshot begins | `{ tenant_id, snapshot_id, trigger }` |
+| `system.db.snapshot.completed` | Snapshot succeeds | `{ tenant_id, snapshot_id, size_bytes, duration_ms }` |
+| `system.db.snapshot.failed` | Snapshot fails | `{ tenant_id, snapshot_id, error }` |
+| `system.db.restore.started` | Restore begins | `{ tenant_id, snapshot_id }` |
+| `system.db.restore.completed` | Restore succeeds | `{ tenant_id, snapshot_id, duration_ms }` |
+| `system.db.restore.failed` | Restore fails | `{ tenant_id, snapshot_id, error }` |
+
+These events can trigger workflows (e.g., notify on failure, run post-restore migrations).
+
+---
+
+## 12) Execution Plan
+
+### Phase 1: Foundations
+
+1. **Migration**: `managed_db_snapshots` table + `ALTER TABLE managed_db_tenants` to add backup columns (`backup_schedule`, `backup_retention`, `snapshot_on_delete`, `snapshot_on_reset`, `last_snapshot_at`).
+2. **TypeID**: Add `generateManagedDbSnapshotId()` to `packages/shared/src/ids.ts`.
+3. **Dockerfile**: Install `postgresql-client-16` in the orchestrator Dockerfile.
+4. **S3 bucket**: Terraform module in infra repo (production). MinIO bucket creation for local k3d (reuse `BucketProvisioner` pattern).
+5. **Snapshot service**: Core `pg_dump` → S3 streaming upload in a shared service (usable by both API and orchestrator).
+6. **DB queries**: Snapshot CRUD in `packages/db/src/queries/managed-db-snapshots.ts`.
+7. **API endpoints**: Create, list, show, delete snapshots.
+8. **CLI commands**: `eve db snapshot` and `eve db snapshots`.
+
+**Acceptance**: Manual snapshot of a managed DB appears in S3, metadata in DB, listed via CLI.
+
+### Phase 2: Restore
+
+1. Restore service: S3 download → `pg_restore` with connection termination and re-grant.
+2. Pre-restore safety snapshot.
+3. `eve db restore` CLI command.
+4. Cross-environment restore support.
+
+**Acceptance**: Restore from snapshot produces a working tenant DB. Safety snapshot exists.
+
+### Phase 3: Scheduling & Automation
+
+1. Manifest schema extension: `backup` block in `ManagedDbConfigSchema` (`packages/shared/src/schemas/manifest.ts`).
+2. Deploy reconciler: read `backup` from manifest, populate `backup_schedule`/`backup_retention`/`snapshot_on_delete`/`snapshot_on_reset` on the tenant row.
+3. Orchestrator cron: `ManagedDbSnapshotScheduler` (registered in `CronModule`).
+4. Snapshot expiry pruner cron: `ManagedDbSnapshotPruner`.
+5. Snapshot-on-delete in reconciler `handleDeleting()`.
+6. Snapshot-on-reset in `EnvDbService.reset()` (`apps/api/src/environments/env-db.service.ts`) — **note**: reset runs in the API process, not the orchestrator, so the snapshot service must be available in both.
+7. `eve db backup-status` CLI command + API endpoint.
+8. `--skip-snapshot` flag on `eve db destroy` and `eve db reset`.
+
+**Acceptance**: Scheduled snapshots fire on cron, old snapshots are pruned, `destroy` creates safety snapshot.
+
+### Phase 4: Observability & Hardening
+
+1. Events for snapshot/restore lifecycle.
+2. Snapshot metrics in analytics (count, total size, failure rate).
+3. Alerting hooks for failed scheduled snapshots.
+4. Manual test scenario: scheduled backup, manual restore, destroy with snapshot.
+5. Documentation updates (system docs, CLI reference, eve-skillpacks).
+
+**Acceptance**: Full DR cycle tested end-to-end. Docs updated. Agents can discover and use snapshot/restore via CLI.
+
+---
+
+## 13) RTO/RPO Targets
+
+| DB Class | RPO (Data Loss Window) | RTO (Recovery Time) | How |
+|----------|----------------------|--------------------|----|
+| `db.p1`  | Up to 7 days (RDS only) | Hours (manual) | RDS instance restore + tenant extraction |
+| `db.p2`  | Up to 24 hours | Minutes | Latest daily snapshot → `eve db restore` |
+| `db.p3`  | Up to 24 hours | Minutes | Daily snapshot (default) or manifest-defined schedule → `eve db restore` |
+| Custom   | Per cron schedule | Minutes | Manifest-defined schedule → `eve db restore` |
+
+Post-restore, app-level migrations may need to run depending on schema drift since snapshot time.
+
+---
+
+## 14) Risks and Mitigations
+
+| Risk | Impact | Mitigation |
+|------|--------|-----------|
+| `pg_dump` of large DB blocks tenant connections | Degraded performance during backup | `pg_dump` uses `ACCESS SHARE` locks only; no write blocking. Monitor duration. |
+| S3 upload failure mid-stream | Orphaned multipart uploads, incomplete snapshot | `AbortMultipartUpload` on failure. S3 lifecycle rule to clean incomplete uploads after 1 day. |
+| Restore to wrong environment | Data contamination | CLI requires `--force` flag. Restore remaps credentials to target tenant. |
+| Snapshot storage costs grow unbounded | Unexpected S3 bill | Retention defaults + pruner cron + S3 lifecycle hard ceiling (90d). |
+| Scheduled snapshot runs during peak traffic | Performance impact | Default schedule at 02:00 UTC (off-peak). Max 2 concurrent snapshots per instance. |
+| Snapshot-on-delete blocks teardown | Slow environment cleanup | Snapshot failure logs warning but doesn't block delete. Best-effort safety net. |
+| pg_dump/pg_restore version mismatch | Restore fails with "unsupported version" | Record `pg_version` in snapshot metadata. Validate compatibility before restore. pg_restore is forwards-compatible (newer tool restores older dumps) but not the reverse. |
+| Tenant deleted before snapshot completes | Orphaned S3 objects, dangling metadata | Pruner handles stale `in_progress` snapshots (>2 hours). S3 lifecycle provides hard ceiling. Snapshot-on-delete runs before DROP. |
+| Concurrent snapshot + restore on same tenant | Data corruption during restore | Restore terminates connections and requires `--force`. Concurrency guard prevents two snapshots. Restore should also check for in-progress snapshots and refuse if one exists. |
+
+---
+
+## 15) Code Organisation
+
+The snapshot/restore logic is needed in multiple services:
+- **API**: Manual snapshots (CLI-triggered), pre-reset snapshots, restore, download/export/import
+- **Orchestrator**: Scheduled snapshots (cron), pre-delete snapshots (reconciler), pruner
+
+To avoid duplication, place the core execution logic in a shared location:
+
+| Component | Location | Why |
+|-----------|----------|-----|
+| DB queries (`managed-db-snapshots.ts`) | `packages/db/src/queries/` | Same pattern as `managed-db.ts` |
+| Snapshot executor (pg_dump → S3 stream) | `packages/shared/src/managed-db/snapshot-executor.ts` | Pure function: takes DB config + S3 config, returns stream |
+| S3 client factory | `packages/shared/src/managed-db/snapshot-storage.ts` | Reuses `EVE_STORAGE_*` env vars from `BucketProvisioner` |
+| Zod schemas (API request/response) | `packages/shared/src/schemas/managed-db.ts` | Extend existing file |
+| API controller + service | `apps/api/src/environments/` | Extend `ManagedDbController` and `ManagedDbService` |
+| Cron services | `apps/orchestrator/src/cron/` | New files alongside existing crons |
+
+---
+
+## 16) Success Criteria
+
+This plan is successful when:
+
+1. App teams can add `backup.schedule` to their manifest and get automated snapshots without writing any code.
+2. `eve db snapshot` and `eve db restore` work reliably for manual DR operations.
+3. Production-class DBs (`db.p2+`) are automatically protected by daily snapshots and snapshot-on-delete.
+4. Snapshot storage is self-managing — old snapshots are pruned, costs are predictable.
+5. The full DR cycle (snapshot → catastrophic loss → restore) completes in under 5 minutes for typical databases.

+# Unified Inference Substrate Plan
+
+> Status: Plan
+> Created: 2026-03-06
+> Related: [private-model-inference-substrate.md](../ideas/private-model-inference-substrate.md), [inference-ollama.md](../system/inference-ollama.md)
+
+## Summary
+
+Replace Eve's Ollama-centric inference system with a **Unified Inference Substrate** architecture. Eve manages the full lifecycle of GPU inference endpoints — provisioning, health monitoring, auto-scale-to-zero, and wake-on-demand — through a provider-agnostic abstraction. RunPod Serverless is the first substrate. Commercial APIs (Together, Fireworks, etc.) remain simple `openai_compat` targets with base URLs.
+
+**Core principle**: Every inference endpoint speaks OpenAI `/v1/chat/completions`. The substrate abstraction handles lifecycle (create, wake, sleep, destroy), not protocol.
+
+---
+
+## Why
+
+1. **Nobody uses Ollama** — the Ollama integration was speculative. No users, no backward compatibility.
+2. **Cost** — GPU instances running 24/7 burn money. RunPod serverless bills per-second with true scale-to-zero.
+3. **Cold starts are solvable** — RunPod FlashBoot gets P90 under 2s. Baked Docker images get sub-5s. Active workers get zero.
+4. **One protocol** — Everything converged on OpenAI-compat. We don't need `ollama_api` as a transport profile.
+5. **Self-service** — Org/project admins should provision their own GPU inference without platform intervention.
+
+---
+
+## Architecture
+
+### Substrate Abstraction
+
+```
+InferenceSubstrate (interface)
+├── RunPodSubstrate      — Serverless GPU endpoints (scale-to-zero, managed lifecycle)
+├── BYOSubstrate         — Any OpenAI-compat endpoint (user provides URL + key)
+└── [future]             — Modal, Beam, Cerebrium, etc.
+
+Each substrate implements:
+  provision(config)    → Creates the endpoint, returns connection details
+  deprovision(id)      → Tears down the endpoint
+  wake(id)             → Ensures endpoint is ready to serve
+  sleep(id)            → Scales endpoint to zero workers
+  health(id)           → Returns structured health status
+  status(id)           → Returns lifecycle state (provisioning, ready, sleeping, error)
+```
+
+### How It Fits
+
+```
+User: eve inference provision --substrate runpod --gpu a100 --model meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B
+  │
+  ▼
+Eve API: Create target (type=gpu_serverless, substrate=runpod)
+  │
+  ▼
+RunPodSubstrate.provision():
+  1. Create serverless template (vLLM + model config)
+  2. Create endpoint (workersMin=0, workersMax=N, idleTimeout=30s)
+  3. Store RunPod endpoint ID in target.substrate_config
+  4. Set base_url = https://api.runpod.ai/v2/{endpoint_id}/openai/v1
+  │
+  ▼
+Target registered: status=ready, base_url set, health probing starts
+  │
+  ▼
+First request: POST /inference/v1/chat/completions { model: "managed/llama-70b" }
+  → Route resolves to target
+  → If no active workers, RunPod auto-scales from zero (0.5-12s cold start)
+  → Response streams back via standard OpenAI format
+  → Usage recorded (tokens, cost, org/project attribution)
+  │
+  ▼
+No requests for idleTimeout seconds → RunPod auto-scales to zero → $0 cost
+```
+
+### Target Types (New)
+
+| Type | Description | Lifecycle | Who Manages |
+|------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
+| `gpu_serverless` | RunPod/Modal/etc. serverless GPU endpoint | Eve provisions + auto-scales | Eve (via substrate) |
+| `gpu_dedicated` | Persistent GPU endpoint (BYO vLLM, pi-pods) | User manages lifecycle | User |
+| `openai_compat` | Any OpenAI-compatible API (Together, Fireworks, custom) | Always-on, no lifecycle | User |
+
+### What Gets Removed
+
+| Current | Status |
+|---------|--------|
+| `ollama_pool` target type | **Delete** |
+| `external_ollama` target type | **Delete** |
+| `ollama_api` transport profile | **Delete** (everything is `openai_compat`) |
+| `InferenceBootstrapService` (creates `platform-gpu` from env var) | **Delete** |
+| `InferenceWakeService` (AWS ASG scale-up) | **Replace** with substrate `wake()` |
+| `eve ollama` CLI commands | **Replace** with `eve inference` |
+| Ollama-specific endpoints (`/pull`, `/remote-models`) | **Delete** |
+| `EVE_OLLAMA_*` env vars | **Delete** |
+| `k8s/overlays/local/app-secret-ollama.patch.yaml` | **Delete** |
+| Ollama GPU AMI, ASG, launch template (in infra repo) | **Decommission** |
+
+### What Stays (Reused)
+
+| Component | Change |
+|-----------|--------|
+| `inference_targets` table | Add `substrate` + `substrate_config` columns |
+| `inference_models` table | No change |
+| `inference_aliases` table | No change |
+| `inference_installs` table | No change |
+| `inference_route_policies` table | No change |
+| `inference_quotas` table | No change |
+| `InferenceService` (routing, admission, usage) | Refactor — remove Ollama specifics, add substrate dispatch |
+| `TargetAdmissionService` | No change |
+| `InferenceHealthService` | Refactor — delegate probing to substrate |
+| Chat completions endpoint | No change (already OpenAI-compat) |
+| Managed model catalog | No change |
+| Protocol bridge registry | Simplify (remove `ollama_api` path) |
+| Token budgets + usage recording | No change |
+
+---
+
+## Database Changes
+
+### Migration: Evolve Target Schema
+
+```sql

+ALTER TABLE inference_targets
+  ADD COLUMN substrate TEXT,            -- 'runpod' | 'modal' | null (BYO)
+  ADD COLUMN substrate_config JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'::jsonb,
+  ADD COLUMN idle_timeout_seconds INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 30,
+  ADD COLUMN substrate_endpoint_id TEXT; -- external ID (RunPod endpoint ID, etc.)
+

+

+
+### `substrate_config` Shape (RunPod)
+
+```typescript
+interface RunPodSubstrateConfig {
+  runpod_endpoint_id: string;      // RunPod endpoint ID
+  runpod_template_id?: string;     // Template used to create endpoint
+  gpu_type: string;                // 'AMPERE_80', 'HOPPER_80', etc.
+  gpu_count: number;               // GPUs per worker
+  model_name: string;              // HuggingFace model ID
+  hf_token_ref?: string;           // Secret reference for gated models

+  workers_max: number;             // Max concurrent workers

+}

+
+---
+
+## RunPod Integration Details
+
+### API Surface Used
+
+| Operation | API | Method |
+|-----------|-----|--------|
+| Create endpoint | `POST https://rest.runpod.io/v1/endpoints` | REST |
+| Update endpoint | `PATCH https://rest.runpod.io/v1/endpoints/{id}` | REST |
+| Delete endpoint | `DELETE https://rest.runpod.io/v1/endpoints/{id}` | REST |
+| Get endpoint | `GET https://rest.runpod.io/v1/endpoints/{id}` | REST |
+| Health check | `GET https://api.runpod.ai/v2/{id}/health` | REST |
+| Chat completions | `POST https://api.runpod.ai/v2/{id}/openai/v1/chat/completions` | OpenAI |
+| List models | `GET https://api.runpod.ai/v2/{id}/openai/v1/models` | OpenAI |
+| Create template | GraphQL mutation `saveTemplate` | GraphQL |
+
+### Authentication
+
+RunPod uses a single API key for all operations. Stored as a platform secret or org-scoped secret:
+
+```
+platform.runpod.api_key    — Platform-wide RunPod key
+org.runpod.api_key         — Org-specific RunPod key (self-service)
+```
+
+The same key authenticates both management API calls (create/delete endpoints) and inference calls (chat completions).
+
+### Cold Start Strategy
+
+Eve's approach to cold starts, from cheapest to fastest:
+
+| Strategy | Cold Start | Cost | Config |
+|----------|-----------|------|--------|
+| **Scale-to-zero (default)** | 2-12s (FlashBoot) | $0 when idle | `workers_min: 0` |
+| **Baked Docker image** | Sub-5s | $0 when idle + image build time | `docker_image: "custom/vllm-model:v1"` |
+| **Warm minimum** | 0s | Continuous billing at discounted rate | `workers_min: 1` |
+
+**Recommendation**: Default to scale-to-zero. Offer `workers_min` as an option for latency-sensitive workloads. Baked images are an optimization users can apply themselves.
+
+### RunPod GPU Types (Reference)
+
+| Eve Name | RunPod ID | VRAM | Flex $/hr |
+|----------|-----------|------|-----------|
+| `a100` | `AMPERE_80` | 80 GB | $2.74 |
+| `h100` | `HOPPER_80` | 80 GB | $4.18 |
+| `h200` | `HOPPER_141` | 141 GB | $5.58 |
+| `l40s` | `ADA_48` | 48 GB | $1.91 |
+| `a6000` | `AMPERE_48` | 48 GB | $1.22 |
+| `4090` | `ADA_24_PRO` | 24 GB | $1.12 |
+| `l4` | `ADA_24` | 24 GB | $0.68 |
+| `a4000` | `AMPERE_16` | 16 GB | $0.58 |

+## Substrate Interface
+
+```typescript
+// packages/shared/src/inference/substrate.ts
+
+export interface SubstrateHealth {

+  jobs_in_queue: number;

+
+export interface SubstrateProvisionResult {

+
+### Phase 2: RunPod Substrate (3-4 days)
+
+**Goal**: Implement RunPod as the first real substrate. Eve can provision, manage, and auto-scale RunPod serverless vLLM endpoints.
+
+#### 2a. RunPod Client

+- Handle authentication via platform/org secrets

+#### 2b. RunPod Substrate Implementation
+- Create `apps/api/src/inference/substrates/runpod.substrate.ts`
+- Implement `InferenceSubstrate` interface:
+  - `provision()` → Create RunPod template + endpoint, set `workersMin=0`
+  - `deprovision()` → Set workers to 0, delete endpoint
+  - `wake()` → Set `workersMin=1` temporarily (or rely on auto-scale if request in flight)
+  - `sleep()` → Set `workersMin=0` (flex workers idle out)

+#### 2c. Provisioning API
+- New endpoints on `InferenceController`:
+  - `POST /inference/targets/provision` — Provision a substrate-managed target
+  - `POST /inference/targets/:id/sleep` — Scale to zero
+  - `GET /inference/targets/:id/substrate-status` — Detailed substrate status
+- `InferenceService.provisionTarget()` → validates config, calls substrate, creates target record
+- `InferenceService.deprovisionTarget()` → calls substrate, deletes target record
+
+#### 2d. Secrets Integration
+- RunPod API key stored as platform secret: `platform.runpod.api_key`
+- Or org-scoped: `org.runpod.api_key` (for self-service)
+- HuggingFace token for gated models: `platform.hf.token` or scoped
+
+#### 2e. Wire Into Chat Completions
+- When `chatCompletions()` routes to a `gpu_serverless` target:
+  - Check health via substrate
+  - If no workers active, request flows to RunPod → auto-scales from zero
+  - RunPod handles the cold start transparently
+  - Response streams back as standard OpenAI format
+  - No special client-side handling needed — RunPod's OpenAI endpoint blocks until ready
+- Update `resolveRouteForModel()` to handle new target types
+- All targets use `openai_compat` transport — eliminate transport branching

+**Tests**: Integration tests against RunPod sandbox (or mocked), provisioning flow, health check mapping.

+## Design Decisions
+
+### Why Not Full Commercial API Integration?
+
+Commercial APIs (Together, Fireworks, Groq) are just OpenAI-compatible endpoints with API keys. No lifecycle management needed — they're always on, they handle scaling, they bill per-token. Eve already supports this via `openai_compat` targets. Adding a "commercial API substrate" would be over-engineering — the substrate abstraction is for things Eve needs to *manage*.
+
+### Why RunPod First?
+
+1. **OpenAI-compat out of the box** — vLLM worker template exposes `/v1/chat/completions` directly
+2. **True scale-to-zero** with per-second billing
+3. **REST API for lifecycle** — no SSH, no custom orchestration
+4. **FlashBoot** — cold starts under 2s at P90 without any custom work
+5. **Price-competitive** — H100 at $4.18/hr flex, $3.35/hr active
+
+### Why Remove Transport Profiles?
+
+The `ollama_api` vs `openai_compat` distinction only existed because Ollama spoke a different protocol. With Ollama gone, everything is OpenAI-compat. Keeping the transport profile column for now (in case a future substrate needs it) but defaulting everything to `openai_compat` and removing the branching logic.
+
+### Wake Strategy for RunPod
+
+RunPod serverless auto-scales from zero when a request arrives. Eve doesn't need to explicitly "wake" a RunPod endpoint — just forward the request and RunPod handles cold start transparently. The `wake()` method on the substrate is there for:
+1. **Pre-warming**: User calls `eve inference target wake` before a burst of requests
+2. **Health transitions**: Detect when workers come online after sleeping
+3. **Future substrates**: Some providers may need explicit wake calls
+
+For RunPod, `wake()` can optionally set `workersMin=1` temporarily, then reset after a timeout.
+
+### Idle Timeout Ownership
+
+RunPod manages idle timeout at the worker level (default 5s). Eve sets this during provisioning and can update it. The `idle_timeout_seconds` on the target record is Eve's configuration that gets synced to the substrate.
+
+**Default**: 30 seconds. This balances cost (only 30s of idle billing per burst) against cold start latency (avoids cold starts for closely-spaced requests).
+

+
+## Adding a New Substrate (Future)
+
+To add Modal, Beam, or any other GPU provider:
+
+1. Create `apps/api/src/inference/substrates/{name}.client.ts` — API client
+2. Create `apps/api/src/inference/substrates/{name}.substrate.ts` — implement `InferenceSubstrate`
+3. Define `{Name}SubstrateConfig` type in `packages/shared/src/inference/substrate.ts`
+4. Register in `substrate-registry.ts`
+5. Add CLI `--substrate {name}` option
+6. Add provider secrets: `platform.{name}.api_key`
+
+That's it. No database changes, no routing changes, no admission changes. The substrate interface insulates the rest of the system.
+
+---
+
+## Risks and Mitigations
+
+| Risk | Mitigation |
+|------|-----------|
+| RunPod API changes | Pin to documented REST v1 API; client has version header |
+| RunPod outage | BYO targets (`openai_compat`) work independently; no substrate dependency |
+| Cold start too slow for interactive chat | Document `workers_min=1` option; show cost trade-off clearly |
+| Secrets leak via substrate_config | Secrets stored as refs (`platform.runpod.api_key`), never inline |
+| RunPod template creation complexity | Pre-define common templates (vLLM + popular models); custom templates for advanced users |
+| Cost surprise | Show estimated $/hr in CLI status; token budgets enforce hourly caps |
+
+---
+
+## Sequencing Recommendation
+
+**Phase 1 + 2 in parallel**: Substrate interface (Phase 1b) can be designed while Ollama cleanup (Phase 1c) proceeds. RunPod client (Phase 2a) can start as soon as the interface is defined.
+
+**Phase 3 after 1+2**: CLI needs the API endpoints to exist.
+
+**Phase 4 anytime**: Observability is incremental and low-risk.
+
+**Phase 5 after staging verification**: Don't touch infra until the new system is proven.
+
+Total estimated effort: **~10-14 days** across all phases.
